Integrated analysis: Apple Watch wearables + CGM + Bloodwork + Symptom Context
Age 34 Male BMI 22.9 Bloodwork: 5 Mar 2026 CGM: 8–15 Apr 2026 Wearables: 90-day window Report: April 2026
Action Plan

Your Next Steps — In Order

Based on your symptoms (6 months of fatigue, constant yawning, poor sleep, stress) combined with your data, here is what to do and when. Everything else in the report supports these five actions.

⚡ Do These First

THIS WEEK
Get a home sleep study / overnight pulse oximetry Your 6-month fatigue, constant yawning, SpO₂ averaging 95.3%, feeling "on alert" at night, and only 10% deep sleep all point to sleep-disordered breathing (sleep apnoea or UARS) as a primary driver. This is the single most likely root cause of your chronic fatigue. Ask your GP for a referral to a home sleep study, or order an overnight SpO₂ monitor. If apnoea-hypopnoea index (AHI) >5: this becomes the #1 treatment priority — CPAP can transform energy levels within 2–4 weeks.
THIS WEEK
Repeat full blood count (CBC) with your GP WBC of 2,800 is 35% below the lower limit and needs confirmation. It may be transient, but must not be ignored. Mention the 6-month fatigue to your GP — EBV/CMV viral titres and a haematology review may be warranted if the leucopenia is confirmed.
WEEK 1
Start D3 (3,000 IU) + K2 (100µg MK-7) + Magnesium Glycinate (300mg at night) Magnesium glycinate is clinically supported for deep sleep improvement — directly addressing your 10% deep sleep. D3+K2 targets your bone resorption and sub-optimal Vitamin D (37.6 ng/mL). Take magnesium at bedtime, D3+K2 with your largest meal. Expect measurable deep sleep improvement in 2–3 weeks.
WEEK 1
Start Methyl-B12 (1,000µg) + Methylfolate (400µg) daily Directly targets your homocysteine of 13.6 µmol/L (optimal <10). Homocysteine at this level raises long-term cardiovascular and cognitive risk — both highly addressable with targeted B vitamins. Expect a 3–4 point drop at your 12-week retest.
WEEKS 1–4
Address the stress-sleep cycle with a structured wind-down protocol Your sympathetic nervous system is chronically activated — feeling "on alert" at night is textbook hypervigilance from chronic stress + probable sleep apnoea. Before sleep results come back: (1) Hard stop on screens 45 min before bed; (2) 4-7-8 breathing or box breathing for 5 min before sleep — proven to shift to parasympathetic within 60 seconds; (3) Consistent bedtime ±15 min every day including weekends; (4) Room temperature 18–19°C. Track HRV — if sympathetic dominance is the driver, expect HRV to improve within 7–10 days of a consistent protocol.
ONGOING
Continue strength training (log it!) + add Zone 2 session 1×/week You're doing 2–3× strength training per week — excellent, and already addressing some of the bone resorption signal. The CTx elevation at 672 pg/mL despite this training volume confirms the issue is primarily hormonal (low E2 from high SHBG), not loading. Keep lifting. Add one 45-min Zone 2 cardio session (conversational pace, ~65% max HR) to drive VO₂max from 45.2 toward 50 mL/kg/min — a meaningful mortality benefit. Your padel and running already partially serve this.
Why You Feel This Way

Connecting Your Symptoms to the Data

Six months of fatigue + constant yawning + stress + poor sleep + feeling hypervigilant at night. These aren't separate problems — they're the same problem expressing through different channels.

⚠ Suspected Primary Driver: Sleep-Disordered Breathing (Sleep Apnoea / UARS)
This is a hypothesis based on your symptoms + data. The home sleep study will confirm or rule it out.
SpO₂ 95.3% avg

Borderline oxygen saturation during sleep. Dips below 94% trigger arousal events — you briefly wake up without knowing, destroying deep sleep architecture.

Deep Sleep Only 10%

Target is 15–25%. Arousal events (from oxygen dips) fragment deep sleep cycles. This is where growth hormone is secreted and repair happens.

Constant Yawning

Yawning is a hypoxic drive — your brain signals for more oxygen. Combined with 6-month fatigue, this is a textbook sign of chronic nocturnal hypoxia.

"On Alert" at Night

Apnoea events trigger cortisol + adrenaline release as a survival response. This creates a state of hypervigilance that feels like you "can't switch off."

Chronic Fatigue (6 months)

Sleep that looks adequate in duration (7.1h) but is fragmented and oxygen-deprived is not restorative. You're spending 7h in bed and not recovering.

Downstream: Hormones + Immunity

Chronic nocturnal stress → cortisol dysregulation → elevated SHBG → suppressed E2 → bone resorption. Also suppresses immune function → contributing to WBC 2,800.

Why Work Stress Compounds This

Your morning cortisol (8.35 µg/dL) is on the lower end of normal — which, combined with 6 months of chronic work stress, suggests your HPA axis is showing signs of wear. The body's stress response is meant to be short-term. When it runs continuously (chronic work demands + disrupted sleep), cortisol output starts to flatten rather than peak normally. This manifests as: difficulty feeling alert in the morning, low motivation, and emotional flatness — which is different from laziness. It's a physiological state. The good news: HPA axis dysregulation is very responsive to consistent sleep, reduced stress load, and targeted lifestyle changes. Your HRV of 62ms shows the autonomic system still has reserve.

Your Actual Exercise Volume Is Appropriate

You're doing 2–3× strength + running + padel per week — this is a good volume. The Apple Watch data only captured 29 sessions in 90 days because not every session is logged. The CTx elevation (672 pg/mL) despite this training confirms the issue is hormonal suppression of bone formation (near-zero E2), not lack of mechanical loading. The strength training is already working as a bone stimulus — the hormonal environment needs to improve to allow the response.

Executive Summary

The Full Picture

Nikhil presents as a highly metabolically healthy 34-year-old with exceptional cardiovascular fitness markers, elite-tier inflammation control, and outstanding glucose stability. The fatigue and poor sleep experience are not reflected in the metabolic markers — which are genuinely excellent — but are instead driven by a specific sleep architecture problem and chronic stress cycle. Fix the sleep, and multiple downstream markers (HRV, hormones, energy, bone metabolism, immunity) are likely to improve simultaneously.

Priority Findings

Areas That Need Attention

Ordered by clinical urgency and intervention leverage.

URGENT · INVESTIGATE

Leucopenia + Neutropenia

WBC: 2,800 cells/µL (ref: 4,300–10,300) — 35% below the lower limit

Your white blood cell count is significantly low, with neutrophils (your first-line infection defence) also below range (ANC 1,028 vs. 2,000–7,000 target). This isn't a lifestyle finding — it requires medical investigation to rule out viral suppression, autoimmune cause, or bone marrow pathology. This is the single finding most deserving of a GP referral. Worth re-testing immediately to confirm it's not a transient dip.

URGENT · HORMONAL

High SHBG + Very Low Estradiol → Bone Risk

SHBG: 63.3 nmol/L (ref: 18.3–54.1) · Estradiol: <5 pg/mL (expected: 10–40 in males)

Three markers create a cohesive pattern: SHBG is 17% above the upper limit, binding and removing free testosterone. Estradiol (E2) is essentially undetectable — highly unusual for a male (normal is 10–40 pg/mL). E2 is the primary driver of male bone health, and with it near zero, bone resorption is predictably elevated: CTx at 672 pg/mL vs. <584 target. Without strength training and with hormonal suppression, bone density is silently declining. This is a three-alarm pattern: high SHBG → low E2 → accelerated bone breakdown.

WATCH · METABOLIC

HbA1c at 5.7% — Prediabetes Threshold

HbA1c: 5.7% (lab classifies as prediabetic) · Fasting glucose: 88 mg/dL · HOMA-IR: 0.65

Context is critical here: your fasting glucose (88 mg/dL), fasting insulin (2.97), and HOMA-IR (0.65 — outstanding insulin sensitivity) all suggest the HbA1c of 5.7% is likely confounded by your slightly low haemoglobin (13.6 g/dL) and PCV (41%) — lower red cell mass can artifactually elevate HbA1c. Your CGM data (mean glucose 5.3 mmol/L, 96% TIR, 17% CV) is genuinely excellent. However, we should not ignore the signal: your haemolytic pattern and the prediabetes range result warrant monitoring.

WATCH · STRUCTURAL

No Resistance Training → Sarcopenia Risk

10 "Functional Strength" sessions in 90 days = avg once every 9 days — insufficient for bone protection

With CTx elevated (bone is being broken down faster than rebuilt), elevated CPK (286 U/L — muscle breakdown signal), and no true progressive overload programme, you're not providing the mechanical stimulus that drives bone formation and muscle protein synthesis. Strength training 3x/week with progressive loading is the most direct intervention for the hormonal-bone pattern. This is not optional given the CTx elevation.

WATCH · SLEEP

Deep Sleep Deficit

Deep sleep: 10% of total — below the 15–25% optimal target

Total sleep of 7.1h is adequate, but deep sleep (slow-wave sleep, SWS) at only 10% is low. Deep sleep is when growth hormone is secreted, muscle repair happens, and memory consolidates. For a 34-year-old, this matters for recovery from training, testosterone production, and cognitive function. SpO2 averaging 95.3% also warrants monitoring — persistent dips below 95% can indicate subclinical sleep-disordered breathing compressing deep sleep architecture.

WATCH · LONGEVITY

Homocysteine & Kidney Markers

Homocysteine: 13.6 µmol/L (optimal <10) · eGFR: 71 mL/min · Creatinine: 1.35 mg/dL

Homocysteine at 13.6 is technically within lab range (5–15) but above the optimal <10 threshold associated with lowest CVD and dementia risk. Driven by relative insufficiency of B12 methylation and folate cycling. The creatinine elevation and eGFR of 71 are likely exercise-related (CPK also elevated at 286) rather than true renal impairment — but worth baseline confirmation with a repeat fasted, post-rest measurement.

STRENGTH · PROTECT

Exceptional Metabolic & Cardiovascular Foundation

hsCRP: 0.18 mg/L · Triglycerides: 57 mg/dL · HOMA-IR: 0.65 · HRV: 62ms · RHR: 52 bpm

These are genuinely elite markers, not just "normal". hsCRP of 0.18 mg/L puts your cardiovascular inflammation risk in the bottom 10%. Triglycerides of 57 mg/dL are near-optimal. HOMA-IR of 0.65 indicates exceptional insulin sensitivity — your cells are highly responsive to insulin signals. HRV of 62ms with RHR of 52 bpm represents outstanding autonomic nervous system health. The foundation is strong — we're optimising and protecting it.

HRV 62 ms + RHR 52 bpm = Strong Autonomic Health

An HRV above 50ms with RHR below 55 bpm for a 34-year-old male indicates excellent parasympathetic tone. This is the nervous system equivalent of being "well-rested and well-trained". Your cardiovascular system is in good shape. The latest 7-day HRV of 60ms is holding steady.

VO₂max & Daily Activity

VO₂max of 45.2 mL/kg/min places you in the Good category for a 34-year-old male. Each 1 MET improvement in VO₂max reduces all-cause mortality risk by ~13% (Mandsager et al., JAMA 2018, 120k person dataset). Steps averaging 14,247/day well exceeds the 7,000-step threshold for cardiovascular mortality benefit. This is a genuine strength.

Sleep Analysis

Sleep Architecture — Adequate Duration, Low Deep Sleep

Sleep is the single biggest lever you can pull for HRV, glucose control, growth hormone, bone repair, and cognitive function — simultaneously.

Target: >97% optimal

Deep Sleep at 10% — Below the 15–25% Target

Deep sleep (slow-wave sleep) is when: growth hormone is secreted (critical for muscle and bone repair), testosterone synthesis is partially driven, glucose is cleared from the bloodstream, and memory consolidates. At 10%, you're likely missing 1+ hours of deep sleep per night compared to optimal. Combined with your bone resorption signal and the strength training deficit, this creates a repair deficit. SpO₂ averaging 95.3% is borderline — if you're experiencing dips <94%, a home pulse oximetry check overnight or a formal sleep study is warranted to rule out sleep apnoea.

Glucose Control — Excellent, With a Hypo Signal

Serious hypo; should be 0%

Hypoglycemic Episodes in a Non-Diabetic — Investigate

A non-diabetic spending 3% of time below 3.9 mmol/L and 1% below 3.0 mmol/L is unusual. Each time glucose drops this low, your body releases cortisol and adrenaline as a counter-regulatory response — disrupting sleep, raising next-day hunger, and creating metabolic stress. Likely causes: exercise-driven glycogen depletion (you're highly insulin-sensitive with fasting insulin of 2.97), extended fasting periods, or meal timing issues. The CGM data (mean 5.3, GMI 5.6%) directly contradicts the blood HbA1c of 5.7% — your actual average glucose is in the optimal zone. The elevated HbA1c is almost certainly confounded by your lower haemoglobin mass.

Exercise Analysis

Workout Pattern — Active but Strength-Training Deficit

29 workouts in 90 days (~1 per 3 days) is a reasonable volume. The mix includes functional strength (10×), running (8×), walking (4×), HIIT (2×), yoga (2×). However, the frequency of structured resistance training is inadequate given the bone health signal — and no heavy progressive loading is evident.

Strength Training Frequency vs Bone Health Need

The research is unambiguous: you need progressive resistance training (compound lifts with increasing load) at minimum 3×/week to counteract elevated bone resorption (CTx = 672, 15% above limit). Current pattern of ~1 functional strength session every 9 days, combined with near-zero estradiol and high SHBG, creates a situation where bone breakdown is chronically ahead of bone formation. This is silent in the short-term and measurable at a DEXA scan. Adding structured strength training is the single highest-leverage intervention for your bone health — above any supplement.

The HRV × RHR scatter shows strong autonomic fitness

The scatter plot (HRV vs RHR) shows your data clustering in the high-HRV, low-RHR quadrant — the "fit and well-recovered" zone. The trend line shows the expected negative correlation: higher HRV days correspond to lower RHR. This is healthy and indicates your aerobic base is solid.

Root Cause Analysis

What Your Data Actually Says — The Convergent Story

When multiple independent data streams point to the same mechanism, the diagnosis becomes clear.

The Hormonal-Bone Pattern (Primary Concern)

Four independent markers converge: High SHBG (63.3) → Low free E2 (<5 pg/mL) → Elevated CTx (672) → No progressive loading. This is a classic pattern seen in lean, highly active males with very low body fat: exercise + low body fat drives SHBG up, which suppresses free testosterone AND estradiol conversion (aromatisation needs adequate T substrate). With E2 near zero, the osteoclast brake is released — bone is being resorbed faster than it's being built. Without intervention, this tracks toward early osteopaenia.

The Leucopenia Signal (Unknown Cause — Must Investigate)

WBC of 2,800 is significantly below range and doesn't fit neatly into the hormonal or lifestyle story. Possible causes: (a) Viral suppression — recent/chronic viral infection (EBV, CMV) can suppress bone marrow production; (b) Autoimmune — though RA factor and CRP are negative, other autoimmune causes exist; (c) Benign ethnic or constitutional neutropenia — more common in people of South Asian descent; (d) Nutritional — B12 (602 pg/mL) and folate (6.34 ng/mL) are low-normal. A repeat CBC in 4-6 weeks is the first step.

The Strong Foundation — What's Working

Your metabolic machinery is genuinely elite: HOMA-IR 0.65 (top 5% insulin sensitivity), hsCRP 0.18 mg/L (near-minimum possible inflammation), triglycerides 57 mg/dL, HRV 62ms, steps 14,247/day. These markers reflect a lifestyle that is already doing the right things for the vast majority of chronic disease risk. The issues to fix are specific and addressable — not a wholesale lifestyle rebuild.

12-Week Action Plan

What to Do — In Priority Order

Immediate (This Week) — Medical

1
Repeat CBC (full blood count) with your GP. A single leucopenia reading could be transient (post-viral, dehydration, sampling error). A repeat in 4-6 weeks will confirm whether this is persistent. If confirmed, request EBV/CMV viral titres and consider haematology referral.
2
Repeat creatinine and eGFR fasted, at rest (no training for 48h prior). Current elevation is almost certainly exercise/CPK-related, but needs confirmation. If eGFR is still <75 at rest, further kidney workup is warranted.
3
Discuss the SHBG + E2 + CTx pattern with an endocrinologist or sports medicine doctor. The E2 of <5 pg/mL with elevated CTx in a young male needs clinical review. Options may include targeted nutritional support (zinc, magnesium), adjusting training volume, or — if confirmed — medical assessment of hormonal support.

Weeks 1–4 — Foundation Fixes

4
Start structured progressive resistance training 3×/week. The protocol that drives bone formation: compound lifts (squat, deadlift, press, row) with progressive overload. Start with a 5×5 or 3×8 programme. This is the primary intervention for CTx reduction and bone preservation. Expected: CTx reduction of 20–30% over 12 weeks with consistent heavy training.
5
Supplement vitamin D3 + K2. Raise from current 37.6 ng/mL to the 50–60 ng/mL LM optimal range. Suggested: 3,000–4,000 IU D3 + 100µg MK-7 K2 daily with the largest meal. K2 (MK-7 form) directs calcium into bone rather than arterial walls — synergistic with your bone health goals. Retest at 12 weeks.
6
Address homocysteine with targeted B vitamin support. Methylfolate (400–800µg) + methyl-B12 (1,000µg) daily. Your B12 is 602 pg/mL (adequate) but your homocysteine is 13.6, suggesting methylation pathway efficiency could be improved. Target: homocysteine <10 µmol/L at next blood draw. Expect 3–4 point reduction in 8–12 weeks.
7
Address hypoglycaemic CGM events. Add a small protein + fat snack (e.g., nuts or eggs) if exercising for >45 minutes fasted. Consider having a pre-sleep protein source (Greek yoghurt, cottage cheese) to stabilise overnight glucose. Track whether time-below-range (<3.9 mmol/L) drops from 3% toward <1%.

Weeks 5–8 — Build & Optimise

8
Deep sleep protocol. Target deep sleep from 10% to 15–18%: (a) Consistent bed/wake times ±15 min; (b) 18–20°C room temperature; (c) No screens 45+ min before bed; (d) Consider magnesium glycinate 300–400mg at bedtime — well-evidenced for deep sleep improvement. Expected outcome: HRV +10–15ms, growth hormone secretion improved, energy levels better.
9
SpO₂ overnight monitoring. If you have any bed partner reporting snoring, or if you wake un-refreshed despite adequate hours, do a formal overnight SpO₂ recording (many Apple Watch models support this). If >5% of readings are <94%, arrange a GP referral for a home sleep study. Untreated mild OSA is a major deep sleep suppressor.
10
Aim to raise VO₂max from 45.2 toward 50 mL/kg/min over 12 weeks. Add 1×/week Zone 2 session (45–60 min at 65–70% max HR — can hold a conversation). Continue 1×/week HIIT. This combination is most efficient for VO₂max improvement. Going from 45 to 50 mL/kg/min reduces all-cause mortality risk by approximately 20%.

Weeks 9–12 — Measure & Adjust

11
Repeat bloodwork at 12 weeks. Priority markers to retest: CBC (WBC + ANC), CTx, SHBG, E2, Vitamin D, homocysteine, creatinine + eGFR, HbA1c.
12
DEXA scan if budget allows. Given the CTx elevation and hormonal pattern, a baseline bone density scan (DEXA) at a local radiology centre gives you a concrete measurement to track. This is the definitive way to know whether the bone resorption signal is translating into measurable density loss.
Testable Hypotheses

Experiments to Run

Hypothesis 1 — Strength Training → CTx

If you add 3× structured progressive resistance training per week for 12 weeks, expect CTx to drop from 672 toward <584 (the upper reference). Bone formation markers (osteocalcin, P1NP) should rise. Measure: repeat CTx at week 12.

Hypothesis 2 — B Vitamins → Homocysteine

If you take methylfolate 400µg + methyl-B12 1,000µg daily for 8 weeks, homocysteine should drop from 13.6 to <10 µmol/L. This directly reduces long-term cardiovascular and cognitive risk. Measure: homocysteine at 12-week bloodwork.

Hypothesis 3 — Vitamin D3 → Bone + Immune

If you supplement 3,000 IU D3 + K2 daily for 12 weeks, Vitamin D should rise from 37.6 to 50–60 ng/mL. Expect improvements in immune cell function (potentially supporting WBC, though leucopenia cause is still unknown), and bone metabolism. Measure: 25-OH Vitamin D at 12 weeks.

Hypothesis 4 — Sleep Protocol → Deep Sleep + HRV

If you implement consistent bedtime (±15 min), 18°C room, + magnesium glycinate 300mg at night, deep sleep should increase from 10% toward 15%+ over 4 weeks. Expected downstream: HRV increases 5–10ms, morning energy improves. Measure: Apple Watch sleep stages.

Hypothesis 5 — Pre/Post-Workout Nutrition → Hypo Events

If you add 20g protein + 15g fat within 30 min before sessions >45 min, and a pre-sleep protein snack on training days, CGM time-below-range should drop from 3% toward <1%. Measure: CGM re-wear after 2 weeks of protocol change.
